name: Saying 'to be' with Suffixes
summary: Turkish has no separate word for 'am/is/are'; it attaches personal endings instead.
explanation:
Turkish does not use a separate verb for 'to be' in the present. Instead it adds a personal ending to the word: Avustralyalıyım (I am Australian), öğretmensin (you are a teacher).

The endings, which obey vowel harmony, are: -(y)im (I am), -sin (you are), nothing or -dir (he/she/it is), -(y)iz (we are), -siniz (you all are), -ler (they are).

To say there is or have, Turkish uses var (there is/exists) and yok (there is not). Param var means 'I have money' (literally 'my-money exists'); param yok means 'I have no money'.
commonMistakes: Looking for a separate word for 'am' or 'is'; the ending does that job.
Forgetting vowel harmony in the ending: mutluyum but öğretmenim.
Using a verb for 'have'; Turkish uses var and yok with a possessive.
Adding the buffer y in the wrong place after a vowel (mutlu-y-um).
Treating yok as a verb; it simply means 'does not exist'.
tip: Learn var (exists / there is) and yok (does not exist) early; together they cover 'to have' and 'there is'.
